title: XFEL Serial Femtosecond Crystallography (SFX)
sub_domain: Single-pulse diffraction from microcrystals at XFEL
source_data_quality_note: Source MD is widefield-fluorescence template (fan-out artifact). Physics below is grounded in canonical literature for this principle.

E.description: XFEL Serial Femtosecond Crystallography (SFX): xfel serial crystallography produces the measurement through a 4-node primitive DAG L.xfel_pulse -> L.microcrystal_diffraction -> L.bragg_indexing -> int.spatial, with multi-angle tomographic integration and photon-shot-noise-limited (Poisson counting). Recovery is posed as a non-convex inverse problem that inverts the forward operator to estimate the scene-side 3D electron density. Difficulty tier delta=10 with effective condition number kappa_eff~32; calibration-level mismatch (hit_rate, detector_dynamic_range, partial_reflection) sets the accuracy floor at the Omega boundary. See the forward_model field for the closed-form imaging equation.
E.forward_model: I_bragg(h, k, l) = |F(h, k, l)|^2 * partial_factor + n; merge thousands of single-pulse patterns

W.regime: Existence of the recovered 3D electron density is guaranteed within the declared Omega bounds. Uniqueness is local rather than global (non-convex landscape); convergence depends on initialisation and priors. Stability is moderately conditioned (kappa_eff ~= 32); hit_rate dominates the stability cliff; detector_dynamic_range and the remaining mismatch parameters contribute higher-order bias terms. Photon-shot-noise-limited (poisson counting) sets the irreducible data-fidelity floor, while TV / wavelet-sparsity / deep priors stabilise recovery at the ill-conditioned end of Omega.

C.solver_class: linear-operator + convex optimisation [CrystFEL, CCTBX-SFX] | linear-operator + deep neural prior [XFEL-Net]
C.error_bound: Data-fidelity bound: ||x_hat - x||_2 <= C1 * sqrt(sigma_signal^2) / sqrt(photon_count). Mismatch-induced bias adds + C2 * |hit_rate| + C3 * ||x - x_s||_1 (best s-sparse / low-rank approximation residual). Iterative solvers achieve q=2 convergence (O(1/k^2)) near the MLE; epsilon_fn captures the monotone quality degradation across declared Omega.
C.complexity: O(H * W * log(...)) per iteration; learned variants: O(H W Z * F_theta_cost) per forward pass
